own_review.issue: KDoc for the new DurationUnit methods still references [TimeUnit.SECONDS]; TimeUnit is JVM-only and not importable in commonMain, so the doc link cannot resolve on non-JVM targets. Should reference DurationUnit.SECONDS.

API doubling: JVM now carries both TimeUnit and DurationUnit overloads of maxAge/maxStale/minFresh with duplicated KDoc. Long-term multiplatform API design concern (this is the maintainer's actual objection); a kotlin.time.Duration-based API or a deprecation plan would avoid permanent duplication.

FORCE_CACHE remains commented out in the expect companion even though maxStale(Int, DurationUnit) is now available in common code, so it could be promoted to all platforms (verified: only jvmMain defines FORCE_CACHE).

commonClampToInt clamps only the upper bound; safe because all callers require(x >= 0) and Duration of non-negative input yields non-negative seconds. Behavior identical to the old private clampToInt; Duration saturation also preserves old TimeUnit.toSeconds overflow semantics. No bug.

Common tests only exercise DurationUnit.SECONDS with tiny values; the new toDuration().inWholeSeconds conversion and clamping path has no common-test coverage with non-second units (JVM-only CacheControlJvmTest covers DAYS/MILLISECONDS via the TimeUnit path).

points.point: KDoc still references [TimeUnit.SECONDS], should be DurationUnit.SECONDS
commonClampToInt lower bound: add explanatory comment
points.note: Acknowledges code is correct; comment-only nitpick.

Coverage suggestion is legitimate, but sub-claim (c) that Int.MAX_VALUE SECONDS 'overflows Long in toDuration()' is false - Duration handles it via millis representation and saturation; no Long overflow.

points.point: FORCE_CACHE still commented out in expect; could be uncommented now that maxStale is multiplatform
points.note: Verified: expect companion has it commented, only jvmMain:181-184 defines it.

points.point: TimeUnit/DurationUnit overload coexistence; consider @Deprecated on TimeUnit variants per existing convention
points.note: Verified the @JvmName("-deprecated_...") convention exists in the file; substantively engages the method-doubling problem the human reviewer raised.

points.point: KDoc [TimeUnit.SECONDS] unresolvable in commonMain on non-JVM targets; change to DurationUnit.SECONDS
points.note: Verified; most precise statement of the issue among candidates.

points.point: Common test only covers DurationUnit.SECONDS; JVM tests cover DAYS/MILLISECONDS but no non-JVM equivalent; add MINUTES/HOURS conversion test
points.note: Verified: CacheControlJvmTest.kt:174,184,204 cover TimeUnit.DAYS and MILLISECONDS; the absence claim is true.

points.point: commonClampToInt lacks underflow branch; rename or add Int.MIN_VALUE branch
Verify decompiled bytecode resolves Integer.MAX_VALUE equivalently to Int.MAX_VALUE (they are the same, so this is fine)
points.note: Vacuous self-answering point; noise but not false.

points.point: KDoc could note value is interpreted in the given DurationUnit before conversion

missed: Human's core point: the API doubling problem - JVM is stuck with both TimeUnit and DurationUnit method sets and multiplatform callers face an awkward split; v5 mentions the coexistence only descriptively, never raises it as a concern.
exceeds: KDoc TimeUnit->DurationUnit fix with multiplatform-resolution reasoning
Cross-checked JVM vs common test coverage gap

qualitative: This PR is clean: it un-comments the expect declarations for maxAge/maxStale/minFresh using DurationUnit, shares the conversion logic in common code, and preserves JVM TimeUnit overloads; I found no correctness bugs, so grading hinges on doc/design/test observations. v4 is the stronger review here: it lands three verified substantive points (broken TimeUnit KDoc reference, the FORCE_CACHE promotion opportunity, and the overload-doubling/deprecation question that mirrors the human maintainer's only comment), marred by one false technical sub-claim about Long overflow in toDuration. v5 opens with the two best-articulated points (the KDoc multiplatform-resolution issue and a verified test-coverage gap, with accurate claims about CacheControlJvmTest) but pads the rest with vacuous nitpicks and, unlike v4, never engages the method-doubling design concern that was the human reviewer's entire feedback, costing it a miss. Neither candidate fabricated absences; all checkable claims in both reviews were accurate except v4's overflow remark. Net: v4 = 3, v5 = 1.
